key_points: Transportation in plants is carried out by specialized vascular tissues: Xylem and Phloem.
Xylem is responsible for the unidirectional transport of water and minerals from roots to leaves.
Phloem facilitates the bidirectional transport of food (sucrose) from leaves to all parts of the plant.
Transpiration is the evaporation of water through stomata, which creates a 'suction pull' to move water upwards.
Respiration in plants occurs through stomata in leaves and lenticels in stems to release energy for cellular work.
examples: Transpiration Pull: Like drinking through a straw, water moves to the top of tall trees.
Symbiotic Relationship: Lichens (Algae providing food and Fungi providing shelter).
Parasitic Plants: Cuscuta (Amarbel) which climbs on other plants to take their nutrients.
Root Hairs: Structures that increase surface area for maximum water absorption.
formulas: Translocation: Transport of soluble products of photosynthesis through Phloem.
exam_tips: Remember 'X' for Xylem and 'W' for Water; 'P' for Phloem and 'F' for Food.
Note that transpiration helps in cooling the plant, similar to sweating in humans.
Identify that during the day, the rate of photosynthesis is higher than respiration.
Understand that water absorption happens through the process of osmosis in root hairs.
